Westus2 and eastus are the dominant regions: across all 12,267 regional resources westus2 holds 25.9% and eastus 17.2% (with centralindia 9.2% and southeastasia 8.8% as secondary footprints), so new workloads should default to westus2 or eastus as the primary region and reserve centralindia and southeastasia as paired DR or geo-expansion targets unless the user specifies otherwise.

Zone-redundant configuration is the exception rather than the rule: of 317 resources exposing properties.zoneRedundant only 26.2% (83) are zone-redundant, indicating the user routinely deploys single-zone resources, so plans should default to single-zone SKUs and only opt into zone redundancy when the workload explicitly requires HA.

Public network access is the prevailing posture: 89.5% (1,154 of 1,289) of resources reporting properties.publicNetworkAccess have it Enabled and 92.9% (627 of 675) of network ACLs default to Allow, so plans should not assume a private-endpoint-first topology and should only introduce private endpoints, NSP, or default-Deny ACLs where the workload explicitly calls for isolation.

TLS 1.2 is the established minimum-TLS baseline: 96.1% (558 of 580) of resources reporting properties.minimumTlsVersion are pinned to TLS1_2, so all new TLS-capable resources in the plan must set minimumTlsVersion to TLS1_2 to stay consistent with the existing security baseline.

Storage accounts follow a hardened-but-public pattern: 99.1% are Standard tier, 99.1% enforce HTTPS-only, 99.8% disable allowBlobPublicAccess, 82.1% disable shared-key access, and 97.5% use the Hot access tier, so new storage accounts should be provisioned as Standard, HTTPS-only, AAD-auth-only, public-blob-disabled Hot accounts unless the workload dictates otherwise.

Storage redundancy is split between local and geo-redundant SKUs: of 459 storage accounts 50.5% use Standard_LRS while 39.7% use Standard_GRS or Standard_RAGRS, so the planner should treat LRS as the cost-default for non-critical data and recommend GRS/RAGRS for any account flagged as holding business-critical or DR-relevant data.

Key Vaults use the Standard SKU with long soft-delete retention but legacy access policies: 97.7% of vaults are Standard SKU, 95.3% keep the 90-day softDeleteRetentionInDays, but only 39.3% have enableRbacAuthorization=True, so new vaults should be Standard with 90-day soft-delete retention and explicitly opt into RBAC authorization to modernize access control rather than perpetuating the access-policy default.

Virtual networks are flat and isolated rather than hub-spoke: 94.3% (200 of 212) of VNets declare a single address prefix, 92.9% (197 of 212) have zero peerings, and 97.2% have DDoS protection disabled, so the planner should default to standalone VNets with a single address space and only introduce hub-spoke peering, multi-prefix address spaces, or DDoS Standard when the workload explicitly requires shared connectivity or internet-facing protection.

Virtual machines standardize on Trusted Launch with general-purpose D-series sizes: 97.2% of VMs use securityType=TrustedLaunch, the top three sizes (Standard_D32ads_v5, Standard_D32ds_v6, Standard_D32as_v4) are all general-purpose D-series with 32 vCPUs, and 95.1% of OS disks set deleteOption=Detach, so new VMs should default to TrustedLaunch on a current-generation D-series size with osDisk deleteOption=Detach to preserve data on VM deletion.

AKS clusters use Azure CNI Overlay with workload identity on a public API surface: 71.4% (15 of 21) of clusters use networkPlugin=azure with networkPluginMode=overlay, 100% have RBAC enabled, 94.4% enable workloadIdentity, and only 1 of 15 enables a private API server, so new AKS clusters should be planned with Azure CNI Overlay, RBAC + workload identity on, and a public API server unless private-cluster isolation is explicitly required.

App workloads favor cost-efficient compute SKUs: across 176 App Service plans the top tiers are Basic B1 (29.0%), Premium P0v3 (17.0%), and Consumption Y1 (14.2%), and 89.0% of container registries are Standard or Basic rather than Premium, so the planner should default web apps to B1 or P0v3, functions to the Consumption (Y1) plan, and ACR to Standard unless premium features (geo-replication, private link, zone redundancy) are explicitly required.

Cognitive Services accounts use the S0 standard SKU with permissive networking: 97.5% of 160 accounts are sku.name=S0, 98.1% have publicNetworkAccess=Enabled, 99.2% set networkAcls.defaultAction=Allow, and only 25.6% set disableLocalAuth=True, so new AI/Cognitive Services resources should default to S0 with public access and key-based auth, and only tighten to private endpoints or AAD-only auth when the workload is sensitive.
